title: The Algorithm of Us
author: Aria Blake
pages:
Chapter 1: Syntax Error

The bell above the door of Brewed Awakenings chimed, a cheerful sound that completely contrasted with Prachi's current mood. She was staring at her laptop screen, the cursor blinking mockingly next to a line of code she had stared at for three hours. It was a Saturday, the coffee shop was packed, and the air was thick with the scent of roasted beans and impending deadlines.

She took a sip of her lukewarm Americano and grimaced. 'Just compile,' she whispered to the machine. It threw back another stack trace of bright red errors. She let her head drop onto the cool surface of the wooden table.

'You missed a semicolon on line forty-two,' a deep, slightly amused voice said from above her.

Prachi's head snapped up. Standing there was a guy holding a tray with a massive iced latte and a blueberry muffin. He had messy dark hair that looked like he'd been running his hands through it all morning, and warm brown eyes framed by wire-rimmed glasses.

'Excuse me?' Prachi said, bristling defensively.

He gestured vaguely with his chin toward her screen. 'Line forty-two. Inside the for-loop. You closed the bracket but forgot the semicolon. The compiler is getting confused on the next line.' He offered a sheepish smile. 'Sorry. I wasn't trying to snoop. I'm just waiting for a table to open up, and I couldn't help but notice you looking like you wanted to throw your laptop out the window.'

Prachi looked down at her screen. She traced her finger to line forty-two. There it was. The glaring absence of a single, tiny punctuation mark. She typed it in, hit compile, and held her breath. The terminal flashed green. Build successful.

She let out a long exhale, a mix of relief and mild irritation at being shown up. 'Well,' she said, looking back up at him. 'I suppose I owe you one. You can take this seat across from me. Unless you're waiting for someone.'

'Just me and my thesis,' he said, slipping into the chair opposite her and setting his tray down. 'I'm Ashish, by the way.'

'Prachi,' she replied, shutting her laptop halfway. 'And thank you, Ashish. You just saved me from a very public meltdown.'

Chapter 2: The Hackathon

Three weeks later, Prachi and Ashish were no longer strangers. The coffee shop encounter had blossomed into regular study sessions. They were complete opposites in their work styles. Prachi was a chaotic whirlwind of sticky notes and half-drank energy drinks, while Ashish was methodical, with color-coded flowcharts and a crippling addiction to matcha lattes.

When the university announced the annual 48-hour Spring Hackathon, it was unspoken but entirely understood that they would team up. They found themselves huddled in a corner of the freezing gymnasium at 2:00 AM on a Saturday, surrounded by empty pizza boxes and the hum of two hundred overworked cooling fans.

'We need a better API for the mapping feature,' Ashish said, rubbing his eyes beneath his glasses. He looked exhausted, the blue light of his monitor casting sharp shadows across his face. But to Prachi, he had never looked more appealing.

'I can build a custom wrapper for the open-source one,' Prachi offered, cracking her knuckles. 'It'll take me about two hours, but it'll be faster than trying to debug the proprietary one.'

Ashish looked at her, his expression softening. 'Are you sure? You've been coding for fourteen hours straight, Prachi. You need sleep.'

'Sleep is for people who don't want to win the grand prize,' she joked, though her eyelids felt like sandpaper. 'Besides, we make a good team. You handle the elegant backend architecture, and I smash the frontend together until it works.'

Ashish reached across the small table, his fingers brushing against her knuckles before lightly gripping her hand. The unexpected contact sent a jolt, much stronger than the caffeine, straight to her chest. 'We do make a good team,' he agreed softly.

For a moment, the chaotic noise of the hackathon faded away. They were pulling together in this tiny bubble of exhausted delirium, and the air crackled with a tension that had absolutely nothing to do with code.

Chapter 3: The Bug in the System

The tension inevitably snapped, but not in the way Prachi had hoped. They won second place at the hackathon, a victory worth celebrating, but the ensuing weeks brought an unexpected distance. Ashish scored a prestigious internship at a major tech firm downtown, and suddenly, his schedule vanished. Coffee dates turned into rushed text messages, and study sessions became nonexistent.

Prachi tried to be supportive, but the absence stung. She missed his dry humor, his quiet confidence, and the way he always knew when she was stuck on a problem before she even asked for help.

One rainy Thursday, she was back at Brewed Awakenings, sitting at their usual table. She was trying to focus on her final project, but her eyes kept drifting to the empty chair across from her. Her phone buzzed. It was a message from Ashish.

'Hey. Swamped at the office again. Can't make it to the library tonight. So sorry. Let's catch up this weekend?'

Prachi stared at the screen, a heavy feeling settling in her stomach. It felt like they were drifting apart, functioning on entirely different operating systems now. She typed out a quick, noncommittal reply and forced herself to stare at her code.

Sometimes, algorithms fail. Sometimes, the edge cases you didn't account for disrupt the entire flow. Prachi felt like an unhandled exception. She packed up her things, the rain drumming softly against the cafe window, resigning herself to the fact that whatever connection they had sparked was just a temporary glitch.

Chapter 4: Compiling Love

The day of the university's final computer science showcase arrived. Prachi stood nervously by her poster board, demonstrating her interactive data visualization project to passing professors. She hadn't seen Ashish in over a week, and her heart gave a painful little twinge every time she scanned the crowded hall.

Just as she was explaining a complex data cluster to a bored-looking freshman, a familiar voice interrupted. 'Fascinating approach to the dataset rendering. Did you use WebGL?'

She turned, her breath catching. Ashish was standing there, wearing a slightly rumpled suit that made him look unfairly handsome. He was holding a small, white pastry box and a large iced Americano—her exact order.

'Ashish,' she breathed, momentarily forgetting the freshman, who wisely slipped away. 'What are you doing here? I thought you had a presentation at the firm today.'

'I did,' he said, stepping closer. He looked nervous, shifting his weight. 'I finished it early. I couldn't miss this. I couldn't miss you.'

He set the coffee and pastry down on the table next to her demo laptop. 'Prachi, I've been an idiot. The internship... it's been overwhelming, and I let it take over everything. But every time I achieved something there, my first thought was that I wanted to tell you. I missed our coffee shop debugging sessions. I missed... you.'

Prachi felt a slow smile spread across her face, the heavy weight of the past few weeks lifting instantly. She crossed her arms, trying to maintain a facade of sternness. 'You're telling me your prioritization array was sorted incorrectly?'

Ashish laughed, the sound bright and warm. 'Exactly. Total logical error on my part. But I'm ready to patch it. If you'll let me.'

He reached out, his hand finding hers just as it had during the hackathon. But this time, they weren't exhausted or delirious. They were wide awake, and the connection was undeniable.

'I suppose I can review your pull request,' Prachi said softly, leaning in until the chaotic noise of the showcase faded away entirely.

'Best commit of my life,' Ashish murmured, right before he kissed her.
